cover.title: THE GEM
cover.subtitle: The one who sees what could be refined — and says it before the heart is visible.
cover.tagline: You don't point things out because you don't care. You point them out because you see what they could become.

profile_scores.caption: High drive and expression with balanced pride, warmth, and stability — you see the spot that could get better, and you say it exactly as it is.

short_summary: You see the last part that could make something better, and you rarely pretend not to see it.
The loneliness comes when people hear criticism, even when what you meant was potential.
What changes next is not becoming less precise. It's letting your heart arrive with the precision.

full_report.headline: THE GEM
full_report.intro: You are someone who says what you see. If something could get better but stops right there, you don't just let it go. You point it out precisely. This report follows the pattern underneath that: why your refinement is often misread as criticism, why that makes people shrink back, and how your care can be heard more accurately.

chapters.title: When You Love
body: You're someone who says what you see. If something could get better but stops right there, you don't just let it go. You point it out precisely.
People call you sharp because of that. They call you picky too. Sometimes they call you cold.
But here's the thing — you're not someone who looks for what's lacking. You're someone who sees the part that could get even better.
It's the same when you love. When you see that just a little more polish would make things so much better, you say it right away. Because you see the potential.
But the people on the receiving end only ever hear it as "you're saying I'm not enough again." Actually, you weren't angry — you were just a little disappointed.
chapters.profile_note: High Drive · High Expression, Balanced Pride · Warmth · Stability — You see the spot that could get better, and you say it exactly as it is.

chapters.title: The Pattern You Keep Repeating
body: You're an attractive person early in a relationship. Because you tell the truth. Because there's no empty flattery. So the other person trusts you.
The problem starts after that. You're not someone who looks for what's lacking. You're someone who sees the part that could get even better.
The problem is the other person doesn't know the difference. They don't feel like you're showing them potential — they feel like they're being criticized for falling short.
So they start trying less. You point that out precisely too: "You don't seem to be putting in effort lately." But that wasn't them losing interest — it was them giving up.
You were accurate, but you missed the most important signal of all.
chapters.highlight: The reason you feel lonely isn't that you love precision. It's that no one ever realized that precision was love.

chart.title: THE NEVER ENOUGH CURVE
chart.description: The pattern where your wish to refine stays the same, while the other person's attempts shrink and you keep pointing out that shrinking.

chapters.title: What You've Gotten Wrong About Yourself

items.truth: That's partly true. But you're not just being honest — you're someone who believes that noticing what could be refined further is itself a form of love. The other person might not know how to take that precision as affection.
items.truth_short: You're not just honest. You see refinement as care, but others may not hear it that way.

items.belief: You should listen to what's true.
items.truth: Well, that's true. But even true things have timing and delivery. Being right and being helpful in that moment are two different things.
items.truth_short: Truth matters, but timing and delivery decide whether it helps.

items.belief: If I let it slide, I lose myself.
items.truth: No. That's not telling you to see less. It's telling you to show, through your delivery, that the precision is interest in their potential, not criticism.
items.truth_short: You don't need to see less. You need to show that the precision comes from potential, not criticism.

chapters.title: The Person You Keep Choosing
body: They're all different people. But strangely, people with a similar texture keep coming into your life.
Someone who settles for "good enough." Someone who could clearly get much better, but stops right there.
That always feels like a waste to you. Not because they lack ability, but because they give up right when just a little more polish would do it.
chapters.highlight: Someone who acknowledges you finds your gaze burdensome. Someone who loves you knows you're seeing their potential.

chapters.title: What Actually Changes Something
body: What you need isn't to see less. That's who you are at your core, and there's no need to dial it down.
What you actually need is to add one more sentence after you point something out.
Don't stop at "this part is a bit lacking." Go all the way to "but I think it'd be really great if you polished it just a little more."
Sounds like nothing, right? But that one sentence is the only signal that separates criticism from potential.
chapters.action_sentence: After precision, add the sentence that shows the heart behind it.

chapters.title: Where You Go From Here
body: You already know how to spot the place that could get better. You know how to say it accurately.
But what you'll learn next is how to show that your gaze comes from seeing potential.
This isn't about being less precise. It's actually the opposite.
It's about your sincerity getting through more accurately.
chapters.transition_note: You've spent a long time learning to see and say where things could get better. Now the next step is delivering the heart behind it more accurately.

chapters.title: The Types You Keep Falling For

items.type_title: Someone Who Settles for Good Enough
items.description: The project is 90% finished, and they say, "That's enough." You immediately see the final 10% that could make it great. That gap keeps pulling at your heart.

items.type_title: Someone Who Chooses Comfort Over Growth
items.description: Whenever things get difficult, they say, "Why make life harder?" You understand the logic, but part of you mourns the version of them that never got refined.

items.type_title: Someone Who Thinks Close Enough Is Fine
items.description: They shrug and say, "It's basically the same thing." For you, the difference between almost and exactly matters. For them, it never does.

items.type_title: Someone Who Doesn't Care Much About Detail
items.description: They only notice the big picture. The tiny adjustments you care about seem unnecessary to them. Yet you know that small differences are often what change everything.

chapters.title: The Warning Signs You've Missed
chapters.intro_note: These signs actually showed up pretty early. It's just that every time, you only pointed them out more precisely.

signal_strength.label: After hearing what you point out, the other person's words gradually shrink
You mistake that for them trying to do better
They reduce their attempts altogether, because the process of refining feels scary
You point that out accurately too, but miss that it's actually a sign of giving up

chapters.core_signal: The other person thinks you're angry, but actually you were just disappointed.

chapters.title: What a Healthy Relationship Looks Like for You

checklist.title: They understand the heart behind your gaze.
checklist.description: They know it's not criticism — it's you seeing potential.

checklist.title: They don't fall apart hearing something true.
checklist.description: They don't get defensive. They listen, and they accept it.

checklist.title: They take precision as affection.
checklist.description: Inside the sharp words, they know there's a heart that sees potential.

checklist.title: Being together makes things more refined.
checklist.description: A healthy relationship refines both sides, not only one side.

chapters.title: What Happens In Your Next Relationship

sections.who_breaks_you: You almost never fail to spot where something could get better. The problem is that gaze always comes across as criticism. Someone who only hears your words as criticism will talk less, try less, and reduce their attempts.
sections.who_steadies_you: The surprisingly good fit is someone who accepts that your gaze is disappointment, not anger. Someone who can ask, "So you were just disappointed, right?" without turning your precision into a fight.
sections.same_pattern_repeats: You might be drawn again to someone who settles for good enough, or someone who doesn't care much about detail. That can't really be stopped. That's just who you are.
sections.next_signs: This time, the sign will show up earlier and smaller: the moment right as you're about to point something out, when their words get just slightly shorter than usual.
sections.try_this: Normally, you would point it out and stop there. This time, after you point it out, add: "But I think it'd be really great if you polished it just a little more." Then watch their reaction.
sections.final_line: Someone who can take it listens without getting defensive. Someone who can't still only hears it as criticism. That reaction is your answer.

action_plan.summary: You already know how to see what could be refined. What changes now is making sure the care behind that precision is visible too.
action_items: After pointing something out, add one sentence that explains the potential you see.
Watch whether the other person hears refinement as care or only as criticism.
Before repeating the same point, check whether they are trying less because they feel defeated.

closing.message: You've spent a long time learning to see and say where things could get better. This report covered why that precision is often misread — and how to let the heart behind it be heard.
closing.cross_sell_cta: Curious what your partner's pattern looks like? See their Cosmic Blueprint.
